ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The paper applies well-established supervised learning (Random Forests) and standard semantic-similarity/cosine features combined with keyword collation to the task of Japanese short-answer scoring—an application-oriented combination rather than a fundamentally new method. The problem (automated short-answer scoring) is well-studied, and the described techniques appear to be an incremental integration of existing ideas rather than a non-trivial methodological advance. While the empirical observations (limits of entailment, issues with zero-heavy labels) are useful for practitioners, they are expected and would not surprise experts familiar with prior work in automated scoring and textual similarity. | B(3): From the summary, the paper applies established supervised learning (Random Forests) and standard semantic-similarity (cosine) plus keyword-matching features to Japanese national-exam style short answers; this is a coherent engineering combination but not a fundamentally new algorithmic idea. Its contribution appears primarily in adapting and empirically evaluating these techniques for a specific exam genre and diagnosing practical failure modes (textual entailment, sparse zero scores), which is useful but incremental. The paper differentiates its system from prior work by feature design and application context, so it would inform practitioners but is unlikely to surprise an expert in automated short-answer scoring.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies well-established supervised learning (Random Forests) and standard semantic-similarity/cosine features combined with keyword collation to the task of Japanese short-answer scoring—an application-oriented combination rather than a fundamentally new method. The problem (automated short-answer scoring) is well-studied, and the described techniques appear to be an incremental integration of existing ideas rather than a non-trivial methodological advance. While the empirical observations (limits of entailment, issues with zero-heavy labels) are useful for practitioners, they are expected and would not surprise experts familiar with prior work in automated scoring and textual similarity.

reviewer_b.justification: From the summary, the paper applies established supervised learning (Random Forests) and standard semantic-similarity (cosine) plus keyword-matching features to Japanese national-exam style short answers; this is a coherent engineering combination but not a fundamentally new algorithmic idea. Its contribution appears primarily in adapting and empirically evaluating these techniques for a specific exam genre and diagnosing practical failure modes (textual entailment, sparse zero scores), which is useful but incremental. The paper differentiates its system from prior work by feature design and application context, so it would inform practitioners but is unlikely to surprise an expert in automated short-answer scoring.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experiments provide useful empirical results on 8 exam items and explicitly discuss limitations (entailment difficulty, ineffective case-grammar features, problems with many zero scores), but the evaluation is narrow and lacks key methodological details needed for reproducibility (no hyperparameters, software/version, or code) and appears to use a small, potentially unbalanced dataset. No clear ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported for the predictor set or Random Forest settings, which weakens claims about which features drive performance. Related work coverage is described as adequate, but overall the study is adequate yet missing important experiments and reproducibility details to fully support its claims. | B(3): The experiments demonstrate promise (exact-match up to 78% and within-one-point 70–95%) but are limited to only 8 items from a single FY2015 trial exam, so evidence is not comprehensive for the range of exam-style items. The paper reports some negative results and limitations (textual entailment difficulty, ineffective case-grammar features, data imbalance issues), and related work coverage is described as adequate; however, no ablation/sensitivity analyses or hyperparameter details and code are reported, which weakens claims about which predictors drive performance and hinders reproducibility. From an assessment-deployment perspective the supervised setup and limited, unbalanced training data raise practical concerns that are not empirically explored in depth.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments provide useful empirical results on 8 exam items and explicitly discuss limitations (entailment difficulty, ineffective case-grammar features, problems with many zero scores), but the evaluation is narrow and lacks key methodological details needed for reproducibility (no hyperparameters, software/version, or code) and appears to use a small, potentially unbalanced dataset. No clear ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported for the predictor set or Random Forest settings, which weakens claims about which features drive performance. Related work coverage is described as adequate, but overall the study is adequate yet missing important experiments and reproducibility details to fully support its claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments demonstrate promise (exact-match up to 78% and within-one-point 70–95%) but are limited to only 8 items from a single FY2015 trial exam, so evidence is not comprehensive for the range of exam-style items. The paper reports some negative results and limitations (textual entailment difficulty, ineffective case-grammar features, data imbalance issues), and related work coverage is described as adequate; however, no ablation/sensitivity analyses or hyperparameter details and code are reported, which weakens claims about which predictors drive performance and hinders reproducibility. From an assessment-deployment perspective the supervised setup and limited, unbalanced training data raise practical concerns that are not empirically explored in depth.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The paper only compares its system output to human ratings (exact-match and within-one-point rates) and does not evaluate against other automated short-answer scoring baselines or recent methods, so key baselines are missing. While it provides reasonable analysis of feature contributions and acknowledges limitations (e.g., entailment, poor performance with many zeros) and has adequate related-work discussion, the experimental comparisons are inadequate for demonstrating superiority or parity with prior automated approaches. For reproducibility and fair benchmarking I would expect experiments against established supervised and semantic-based scoring systems using the same data and metrics. | B(2): The paper only compares its system to human ratings rather than to existing automated short-answer scoring baselines (e.g., LSA/LSI, BLEU/ROUGE-style baselines, or recent neural/semantic entailment approaches), so key comparative baselines are missing. Although related work coverage is reported as adequate, the experimental comparisons are inadequate to show relative advantage: same dataset and metrics are used versus humans, but no fair head-to-head against prior automated methods is presented. The authors acknowledge limitations (entailment, data imbalance), but do not demonstrate whether prior techniques perform comparably or better on these items, which weakens claims of novelty and practical impact.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper only compares its system output to human ratings (exact-match and within-one-point rates) and does not evaluate against other automated short-answer scoring baselines or recent methods, so key baselines are missing. While it provides reasonable analysis of feature contributions and acknowledges limitations (e.g., entailment, poor performance with many zeros) and has adequate related-work discussion, the experimental comparisons are inadequate for demonstrating superiority or parity with prior automated approaches. For reproducibility and fair benchmarking I would expect experiments against established supervised and semantic-based scoring systems using the same data and metrics.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper only compares its system to human ratings rather than to existing automated short-answer scoring baselines (e.g., LSA/LSI, BLEU/ROUGE-style baselines, or recent neural/semantic entailment approaches), so key comparative baselines are missing. Although related work coverage is reported as adequate, the experimental comparisons are inadequate to show relative advantage: same dataset and metrics are used versus humans, but no fair head-to-head against prior automated methods is presented. The authors acknowledge limitations (entailment, data imbalance), but do not demonstrate whether prior techniques perform comparably or better on these items, which weakens claims of novelty and practical impact.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The topic—automated short-answer scoring combining keyword collation and semantic similarity—is squarely within NLP/ML conference scope and would interest the venue’s audience. However, from the summary the work is heavily applied on a very small set (8 items from one 2015 trial), uses standard Random Forests without reported hyperparameters or code, and lacks reproducibility details and stronger baselines/ablation analyses that top venues expect. Given appropriate expansion of data, more rigorous experimental reporting, and deeper analysis of failure modes, this would be a stronger fit; as presented it is borderline appropriate. | B(3): The paper addresses an applied NLP problem (automated short-answer scoring) that is relevant to top NLP/ML venues, and uses standard supervised methods and expected evaluation metrics (exact-match and within-one-point) with human rater comparison. However, the contribution appears incremental—Random Forests with degree-of-fit and cosine similarity on a small, narrow dataset (8 items from a single Gakken trial) and limited discussion of generalization, hyperparameters, or code release reduces appeal for a broad ML audience. The human-in-the-loop scoring-support framing and explicit limitations (entailment difficulty, sparse/imbalanced supervision) are valuable for educational-assessment researchers but the limited scope and dataset make the fit to a top-tier NLP/ML conference borderline without stronger evidence of generalization or novel methods.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic—automated short-answer scoring combining keyword collation and semantic similarity—is squarely within NLP/ML conference scope and would interest the venue’s audience. Given appropriate expansion of data, more rigorous experimental reporting, and deeper analysis of failure modes, this would be a stronger fit; as presented it is borderline appropriate.

However, the contribution appears incremental—Random Forests with degree-of-fit and cosine similarity on a small, narrow dataset (8 items from a single Gakken trial) and limited discussion of generalization, hyperparameters, or code release reduces appeal for a broad ML audience. The human-in-the-loop scoring-support framing and explicit limitations (entailment difficulty, sparse/imbalanced supervision) are valuable for educational-assessment researchers but the limited scope and dataset make the fit to a top-tier NLP/ML conference borderline without stronger evidence of generalization or novel methods.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles an important applied problem—automating short-answer scoring for high-stakes national exam-style tests—and demonstrates a practical hybrid approach (keyword collation + semantic similarity with Random Forests) that achieves promising within-one-point rates (≈70–95%) on 8 social-studies items. However, its impact is constrained by a small, narrow dataset (only 8 items from a single trial), missing reproducibility details (no hyperparameters or code reported), and acknowledged limits on deep textual entailment and imbalanced/zero-heavy labels, so it is useful and likely to inform follow-up work but not transformative for the field. | B(3): The paper tackles an important, practical problem—automating short-answer scoring for national exam–style items—and demonstrates a usable human-in-the-loop system combining keyword collocation and semantic-similarity features with Random Forests that achieves encouraging within-one-point rates (≈70–95%) on some items. However, the evaluation is limited to eight items from a single Gakken trial exam and the authors report substantive limitations (difficulty with textual entailment, ineffective case-grammar features, and sensitivity to many-zero or unbalanced training labels), so the results are useful and likely to inform practitioners and incremental research but are not yet transformative or broadly generalizable. Overall, this is a moderate-impact contribution that could be built upon for deployment studies and work addressing entailment and dataset balance.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important applied problem—automating short-answer scoring for high-stakes national exam-style tests—and demonstrates a practical hybrid approach (keyword collation + semantic similarity with Random Forests) that achieves promising within-one-point rates (≈70–95%) on 8 social-studies items. However, its impact is constrained by a small, narrow dataset (only 8 items from a single trial), missing reproducibility details (no hyperparameters or code reported), and acknowledged limits on deep textual entailment and imbalanced/zero-heavy labels, so it is useful and likely to inform follow-up work but not transformative for the field.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important, practical problem—automating short-answer scoring for national exam–style items—and demonstrates a usable human-in-the-loop system combining keyword collocation and semantic-similarity features with Random Forests that achieves encouraging within-one-point rates (≈70–95%) on some items. However, the evaluation is limited to eight items from a single Gakken trial exam and the authors report substantive limitations (difficulty with textual entailment, ineffective case-grammar features, and sensitivity to many-zero or unbalanced training labels), so the results are useful and likely to inform practitioners and incremental research but are not yet transformative or broadly generalizable. Overall, this is a moderate-impact contribution that could be built upon for deployment studies and work addressing entailment and dataset balance.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper states clear, testable claims and presents experimental results showing exact-match and within-one-point rates on 8 Gakken exam items, so there is empirical support for the core claim. However, experimental methodology lacks critical reproducibility details (no hyperparameters, random seeds, code, or dataset splits), no comparisons to other automated baselines or ablations beyond noting ineffective features, and no statistical significance testing — all of which weaken confidence in the results. There are no mathematical derivations to assess, and the authors acknowledge practical limitations (entailment, zero-heavy labels, small/unbalanced training data) that point to logical gaps in generalizability. Overall the conclusions are directionally justified by the presented experiments but limited by insufficient methodological rigor and missing analyses. | B(3): The paper presents an implementable Random-Forest approach using degree-of-fit and cosine semantic-similarity features and supports its main empirical claims with item-level exact-match and within-one-point rates on the Gakken FY2015 data. However, the experimental methodology is limited: only eight items from a single trial exam were used, no alternative automated baselines or ablation/stability analyses (e.g., hyperparameters, cross-validation details, statistical significance) are reported, and code/hyperparameters are not provided, which weakens reproducibility and confidence in generalization. The authors acknowledge realistic limitations (textual entailment difficulty, poor performance with many zeros, ineffective case-grammar features), but some claims about system utility for human-in-the-loop scoring are not fully supported by rigorous statistical evidence or broader evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear, testable claims and presents experimental results showing exact-match and within-one-point rates on 8 Gakken exam items, so there is empirical support for the core claim. However, experimental methodology lacks critical reproducibility details (no hyperparameters, random seeds, code, or dataset splits), no comparisons to other automated baselines or ablations beyond noting ineffective features, and no statistical significance testing — all of which weaken confidence in the results. There are no mathematical derivations to assess, and the authors acknowledge practical limitations (entailment, zero-heavy labels, small/unbalanced training data) that point to logical gaps in generalizability. Overall the conclusions are directionally justified by the presented experiments but limited by insufficient methodological rigor and missing analyses.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents an implementable Random-Forest approach using degree-of-fit and cosine semantic-similarity features and supports its main empirical claims with item-level exact-match and within-one-point rates on the Gakken FY2015 data. However, the experimental methodology is limited: only eight items from a single trial exam were used, no alternative automated baselines or ablation/stability analyses (e.g., hyperparameters, cross-validation details, statistical significance) are reported, and code/hyperparameters are not provided, which weakens reproducibility and confidence in generalization. The authors acknowledge realistic limitations (textual entailment difficulty, poor performance with many zeros, ineffective case-grammar features), but some claims about system utility for human-in-the-loop scoring are not fully supported by rigorous statistical evidence or broader evaluation.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Based on the provided summary the paper appears well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (a combined keyword/semantic system and RF predictors for short-answer scoring). However the summary lacks mention of consistent, well-defined notation and does not indicate whether figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate results, which suggests minor presentation gaps. Overall the writing quality is described as adequate but some sections (e.g., experimental details like hyperparameters and figures) appear under-documented and would benefit from clearer, more explicit presentation for reproducibility. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from problem motivation through methods, experiments, and conclusions, with the main contributions and limitations stated clearly early on. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent enough for a reader to follow the Random Forest + semantic-similarity approach, and the writing quality is adequate with related work coverage noted as sufficient. Minor presentation gaps remain (e.g., some experimental details such as hyperparameters and code availability are not reported in the summary), but these are issues of completeness rather than fundamental clarity.

reviewer_a.justification: Based on the provided summary the paper appears well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (a combined keyword/semantic system and RF predictors for short-answer scoring). However the summary lacks mention of consistent, well-defined notation and does not indicate whether figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate results, which suggests minor presentation gaps. Overall the writing quality is described as adequate but some sections (e.g., experimental details like hyperparameters and figures) appear under-documented and would benefit from clearer, more explicit presentation for reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from problem motivation through methods, experiments, and conclusions, with the main contributions and limitations stated clearly early on. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent enough for a reader to follow the Random Forest + semantic-similarity approach, and the writing quality is adequate with related work coverage noted as sufficient. Minor presentation gaps remain (e.g., some experimental details such as hyperparameters and code availability are not reported in the summary), but these are issues of completeness rather than fundamental clarity.

main_claims: Developed an automated Japanese short-answer scoring and scoring-support system for national exam-style written tests that combines superficial keyword collation and semantic similarity measures and returns suggested scores for human raters to revise.

Used Random Forests trained on human-scored data with predictors including degree-of-fit features and cosine semantic similarity between student answers, model answers, and test items to predict multi-level scores.

In experiments on 8 social-studies test items from a Gakken nationwide trial examination (FY2015), automated scores matched human scores exactly for some items (best 78%) and were within one point for about 70–95% of cases when high semantic judgment was not required.

Identified limitations: deep textual entailment remains difficult, case-grammar agreement features were not effective, and supervised learning is hindered when many human scores are zero or when training data are insufficient or unbalanced.
_summary.methodology: Supervised machine learning using Random Forests with predictors that include degree-of-fit features and semantic (cosine) similarity between student answers, model answers, and test-item sentences; the system generates automated scores which are then to be reviewed/adjusted by human raters.
datasets: Gakken nation-wide trial examination (fiscal year 2015) — 8 test items in world history, Japanese history, and geography
metrics: exact-match rate (prediction identical to human rating)
probability that automated estimate differed from human rating by ≤1 point (within-one-point rate)
limitations: Recognizing textual entailment and correct meaning between model and written answers remains technically difficult.
Case-grammar (agreement of case elements) features did not contribute effectively as predictors.
Supervised learning performs poorly when many examinee answers receive zero scores; a large, well-balanced set of human scores is required.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - automated short-answer scoring / educational assessment

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Methods Specialist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a decade of experience designing reproducible NLP experiments and building evaluation pipelines for educational technologies; has published and reviewed papers on automated scoring and assessment.
traits: expects clear, reproducible experimental setup (data splits, random seeds, hyperparameters, software/versions)
looks for appropriate baselines, ablation studies, and statistical significance of reported results
prioritizes rigorous error analysis (confusion matrices, per-class metrics, failure cases) and robustness checks
values clear descriptions of feature extraction, preprocessing, and model selection so results can be replicated
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on methodological rigor and empirical validity, asking for missing experiments or clarifications that would allow replication and fair comparison.

reviewer_b.name: Educational NLP / Impact Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor researching automated assessment and NLP for education, with expertise in short-answer scoring, human-in-the-loop systems, and deployment in K-12 and national exam contexts.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior automated scoring and semantic similarity work and how the contribution advances practice
evaluates pedagogical usability, assessor workflows, and human–AI interaction implications (e.g., suggested scores, reviewer adjustments)
notices dataset provenance, ethical considerations, fairness across student groups, and limitations in real-world deployment
looks for clear discussion of where the approach succeeds/fails pedagogically (types of items suitable for automation) and recommendations for practitioners
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Emphasizes the work's relevance, novelty, and practical impact on educational assessment, asking for contextualization, deployment considerations, and evidence that claims transfer beyond the study setting.
